title: Attenuation of Campylobacter jejuni virulence-associated phenotypes upon loss of rRNA modifications in its small ribosomal subunit.
author_string: Murawska, Małgorzata, Milewska-Puchała, Małgorzata, Skrzypiec-Nowak, Filip, Oleksiak, Jakub, Majewska, Karolina et al.
abstract: The Gram-negative bacterial pathogen Campylobacter jejuni is globally distributed, and a major etiological agent of human gastroenteritis. The various stages of its virulent lifestyle necessitate the ability to survive in the external environment, including in the form of a biofilm, before being ingested by a host and enduring the journey through the gut to attach upon and invade the intestinal epithelium. Evasion of the host immune system, and establishing itself within the epithelial tissue is accompanied by production of toxins and effecting the symptoms of pathogenesis. Each of these stages is regulated by changes in the production of sub-sets of C. jejuni proteins. Here, we have engineered the ribosomal RNA machinery of C. jejuni to interfere with its protein synthesis and transition through the various stages of pathogenesis. This has been achieved by identifying and individually inactivating seven methyltransferases that specifically modify C. jejuni 16S rRNA. Changes in the pathogen's ability to form biofilms, its motility, and attachment and internalisation into epithelial cells are correlated with changes in its protein contents analysed by mass spectrometric proteomics. Each of the rRNA methylations effects protein synthesis in subtly different ways, and together reveal sets of proteins involved in the various stages of pathogenesis. In particular, the roles of proteins involved in the structure, activity and regulation of flagella are linked to the pathogen's ability to form biofilms, its motility and internalisation into epithelial cells.
